title: Preventing Back-Reflection Damage in CW Fiber Lasers
content:
## What is Back-Reflection?

Back-reflection occurs when laser light bounces off the workpiece surface and travels back through the fiber delivery system into the laser source. This is particularly common when processing highly reflective materials like aluminum, copper, brass, and stainless steel at certain angles.

## Why is it Dangerous?

Excessive back-reflection can:
- Damage pump diodes
- Degrade fiber components
- Cause the E-BR alarm to trigger (protective shutdown)
- In severe cases, permanently damage the laser source

## Prevention Strategies

### 1. Optimize Cutting Parameters
- **Increase cutting speed** — faster cuts reduce the time the beam dwells on molten metal
- **Adjust focus position** — slightly defocused beams reduce reflection intensity
- **Use ramped piercing** — gradually increase power during pierce to avoid sudden reflection

### 2. Use Appropriate Assist Gas
- **Nitrogen** at high pressure (12-16 bar) for aluminum
- **Oxygen** creates an oxide layer that absorbs more light
- Never use air for reflective materials

### 3. Material Preparation
- Remove protective film before cutting
- Ensure material is flat and properly supported
- Avoid cutting at steep angles

### 4. Protective Optics
- Always use a protective window in the cutting head
- Replace protective windows regularly (check for burn marks)
- Keep all optics clean — contamination increases scattered reflection

## Raycus Built-in Protection

All Raycus CW fiber lasers include multiple anti-high-reflection mechanisms:
- Real-time back-reflection monitoring
- Automatic power reduction when reflection exceeds threshold
- E-BR alarm with automatic shutdown for extreme cases
- Configurable reflection sensitivity via control interface

## Related Error Codes
- **E-BR**: Back-reflection alarm triggered — laser shutdown
- **W-PLOW**: May indicate optic damage from past reflection events

title: Cooling System Requirements and Troubleshooting
content:
## Cooling Requirements by Power Level

| Power Range | Cooling Type | Min Flow Rate | Water Temp |
|---|---|---|---|
| < 500W | Air cooled | N/A | N/A |
| 500W–2kW | Water cooled | 4 L/min | 20–25°C |
| 2kW–6kW | Water cooled | 8 L/min | 20–25°C |
| 6kW–12kW | Water cooled | 15 L/min | 18–24°C |
| 12kW+ | Water cooled | 25+ L/min | 18–22°C |

## Water Quality Requirements

- Use **deionized or distilled water** with appropriate antifreeze additive
- Conductivity: < 20 μS/cm
- pH: 6.5–7.5
- Replace coolant every **3 months**
- Never use tap water — mineral deposits will clog cooling channels

## Common Cooling Issues

### High Temperature Alarms (E-T1H, E-T2H, E-T3H, E-T4H)

**Causes:**
1. Chiller not running or undersized
2. Ambient temperature too high (> 40°C)
3. Blocked or kinked cooling lines
4. Dirty heat exchanger / chiller filter
5. Insufficient flow rate

**Fix:** Check chiller operation → verify water temperature → inspect flow rate → clean filters → check for blockages

### Condensation Issues (W-HUMID)

**Causes:**
- Chiller water temperature set too low relative to ambient
- High humidity environment (> 70%)

**Fix:** Increase chiller water temperature by 2–3°C above dew point. The rule: **water temp should always be above the room's dew point temperature**.

### Low Flow Warning (W-FLOW)

**Causes:**
- Water pump degradation
- Partially blocked cooling circuit
- Air bubbles in cooling system

**Fix:** Check pump → bleed air from system → flush cooling circuit → verify all valves are fully open

title: MOPA Fiber Laser: Color Marking Guide
content:
## How Color Marking Works

MOPA fiber lasers create colors on stainless steel by forming a thin oxide layer on the surface. Different oxide thicknesses produce different colors through thin-film interference — the same principle that creates rainbow colors on oil films.

## Key Parameters for Color Control

The critical advantage of MOPA over Q-switched lasers is **tunable pulse width**. This gives you precise control over the heat input:

| Parameter | Effect on Color |
|---|---|
| Pulse width (ns) | Primary color selector — shorter = lighter, longer = darker |
| Frequency (kHz) | Pulse overlap — affects uniformity and saturation |
| Power (%) | Heat input intensity — fine-tunes shade |
| Speed (mm/s) | Energy density per area — affects depth |
| Line spacing (mm) | Fill density — affects coverage uniformity |

## Color Recipe Starting Points

*Stainless Steel 304, RFL-P60MX at 60W:*

| Color | Pulse Width | Frequency | Power | Speed | Spacing |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gold/Yellow | 20 ns | 200 kHz | 40% | 1000 mm/s | 0.01 mm |
| Blue | 100 ns | 300 kHz | 50% | 800 mm/s | 0.01 mm |
| Purple | 150 ns | 250 kHz | 45% | 900 mm/s | 0.01 mm |
| Green | 200 ns | 350 kHz | 55% | 700 mm/s | 0.01 mm |
| Red/Orange | 50 ns | 150 kHz | 35% | 1200 mm/s | 0.01 mm |
| Black | 350 ns | 400 kHz | 70% | 500 mm/s | 0.005 mm |

**Note:** These are starting points. Exact parameters vary by material grade, surface finish, and environmental conditions. Always test on sample material first.

## Tips for Consistent Results

1. **Clean the surface** — oils and fingerprints cause color inconsistency
2. **Control environment** — temperature changes affect oxide formation
3. **Use consistent focus** — even small focus changes shift colors
4. **Mark in one direction** — bidirectional marking may show slight color differences
5. **Save proven recipes** — document exact parameters for each color/material combination

title: QBH Fiber Connector: Handling and Cleaning Procedures
content:
## Why Connector Cleanliness Matters

The QBH fiber connector is the interface between the laser source and the delivery fiber. Any contamination on the fiber end face or connector surfaces will:
- Absorb laser energy and generate heat
- Cause power loss (W-PLOW alarm)
- Burn the fiber end face permanently
- In worst case, destroy the connector and require fiber replacement ($$$)

## Cleaning Procedure

### Required Tools
- Lint-free lens cleaning tissues
- Isopropyl alcohol (IPA), > 99% purity
- Inspection microscope (20x–100x)
- Compressed nitrogen (NOT compressed air — it contains oil)
- Anti-static wrist strap

### Steps

1. **Power off** the laser completely and wait 5 minutes
2. **Disconnect** the QBH connector carefully — pull straight, never twist
3. **Inspect** the fiber end face under microscope
   - Clean surface = uniform bright circle, no spots
   - Contaminated = dark spots, burn marks, particles
4. **Clean** by wiping the end face with IPA-dampened lens tissue in ONE direction
5. **Dry** immediately with a dry lens tissue
6. **Re-inspect** under microscope — repeat if needed
7. **Protect** — immediately replace the dust cap or reconnect

## Never Do This

- Never touch the fiber end face with fingers
- Never blow on the connector with your mouth (moisture!)
- Never use compressed air (contains oil mist)
- Never clean with acetone (leaves residue)
- Never reconnect a dirty connector — you'll burn the other side too
- Never leave the connector uncapped for more than 30 seconds

## Inspection Schedule

| Usage | Inspection Frequency |
|---|---|
| Light (< 4 hrs/day) | Monthly |
| Medium (4–8 hrs/day) | Bi-weekly |
| Heavy (> 8 hrs/day) | Weekly |
| After any alarm | Immediately |

title: ABP Fiber Laser: Beam Profile Selection Guide
content:
## Understanding Beam Profiles

Raycus ABP (Adjustable Beam Profile) lasers use a patented fiber combiner to deliver three distinct beam modes through a single output fiber:

### Gaussian (Center Only)
- High energy density at the center
- Best for: **thin sheet cutting** (< 6mm), precision piercing, fine welding
- Characteristics: Small spot size, deep penetration, narrow kerf

### Ring (Ring Only)
- Energy distributed in an annular ring pattern
- Best for: **thick sheet cutting** (> 12mm), wide weld seams, surface treatment
- Characteristics: Wider heat distribution, more stable melt pool, less spatter

### Mixed (Center + Ring)
- Combination of center spot and ring pattern
- Best for: **medium thickness** (6–12mm), versatile processing
- Characteristics: Balance of penetration and heat distribution
- Ratio is adjustable by varying center/ring power split

## Recommended Profiles by Application

| Application | Material | Thickness | Profile | Center/Ring Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cutting | Mild steel | < 6mm | Gaussian | 100/0 |
| Cutting | Mild steel | 6–12mm | Mixed | 60/40 |
| Cutting | Mild steel | > 12mm | Ring | 0/100 |
| Cutting | Stainless | < 4mm | Gaussian | 100/0 |
| Cutting | Aluminum | Any | Mixed | 70/30 |
| Welding | Butt joint | Thin | Gaussian | 100/0 |
| Welding | Lap joint | Any | Ring | 0/100 |
| Welding | Fillet | Any | Mixed | 50/50 |

## Switching Between Profiles

Profile switching is done via the control interface (Ethernet/RS232) and takes effect within **< 10 ms** — fast enough for on-the-fly switching during processing.

The ABP system supports per-layer programming in most CNC controllers, allowing automatic profile changes between pierce, lead-in, and cutting phases.

title: Fiber Laser Safety: Essential Guidelines
content:
## Laser Classification

All Raycus fiber lasers are **Class 4** laser products — the highest hazard class. Direct or scattered radiation can cause:
- **Eye damage**: Permanent blindness from direct or reflected beam
- **Skin burns**: Severe burns from beam exposure
- **Fire hazard**: Can ignite combustible materials

## Required Safety Measures

### 1. Laser Safety Enclosure
- The laser processing area **must** be fully enclosed
- Enclosure must block 1080nm (or appropriate wavelength) radiation
- All access doors must have safety interlocks connected to the laser's INTLK pins
- Interlocks must cut laser emission within < 1 second of door opening

### 2. Personal Protective Equipment
- **Laser safety glasses** rated for OD6+ at 1060-1100nm (for fiber lasers)
- Must be worn by ALL personnel in the laser area, even with enclosure
- Replace glasses if scratched or damaged — damaged filters may not protect

### 3. Warning Signs and Lights
- Post "DANGER — CLASS 4 LASER" signs at all entrances
- Install a warning light (typically amber/red) that illuminates when laser is active
- Mark the beam path zone clearly

### 4. Fire Safety
- Keep a CO2 fire extinguisher within reach
- Remove combustible materials from the processing area
- Ensure adequate fume extraction — cutting produces toxic fumes

### 5. Training
- All operators must complete laser safety training before use
- Designate a Laser Safety Officer (LSO) for your facility
- Keep training records on file

## Emergency Procedures

1. **Emergency stop**: Hit the E-STOP button immediately
2. **Eye exposure**: Do NOT rub — seek immediate medical attention
3. **Skin burn**: Cool with running water, seek medical attention
4. **Fire**: Use CO2 extinguisher, evacuate if uncontrolled

title: Pulse Laser Cleaning: Parameters and Applications
content:
## How Laser Cleaning Works

High-power pulsed fiber lasers remove surface contaminants through a combination of:
- **Thermal ablation**: Rapid heating vaporizes surface layers
- **Photomechanical effect**: Shock waves from rapid expansion detach particles
- **Photochemical effect**: UV-generated ozone breaks down organic compounds

The key advantage: the laser selectively removes contaminants while leaving the base material undamaged.

## Recommended Raycus Models for Cleaning

| Application | Recommended Model | Power |
|---|---|---|
| Light rust, thin paint | RFL-P200 | 200W |
| Medium rust, coatings | RFL-P300, RFL-P500 | 300–500W |
| Heavy rust, thick paint | RFL-P500, RFL-P1000 | 500–1000W |
| Industrial scale removal | RFL-P1000, RFL-P2000 | 1000–2000W |

## Parameter Guidelines

### Rust Removal (Mild Steel)
- Pulse frequency: 20–50 kHz
- Scan speed: 3000–8000 mm/s
- Power: 70–100%
- Line spacing: 0.03–0.05 mm
- Passes: 1–3 depending on rust depth

### Paint Stripping
- Pulse frequency: 20–30 kHz
- Scan speed: 5000–10000 mm/s
- Power: 50–80% (start low to avoid substrate damage)
- Line spacing: 0.02–0.04 mm
- Passes: 1–2 per coat

### Weld Preparation
- Pulse frequency: 30–50 kHz
- Scan speed: 4000–6000 mm/s
- Power: 60–90%
- Clean 10–20mm beyond weld zone

## Tips

1. **Always test** on a sample area first
2. **Wear appropriate PPE** — ablated material becomes airborne
3. **Use fume extraction** — vaporized coatings may be toxic
4. **Check surface after cleaning** — use white light inspection
5. **Multiple light passes** are better than one aggressive pass

title: Choosing the Right Fiber Laser for Your Application
content:
## Laser Type Selection

### CW (Continuous Wave) — For Cutting & Welding
Use when you need sustained high power for:
- Sheet metal cutting (1mm to 60mm+)
- Continuous seam welding
- Cladding and surface treatment

**Choose Global-CE** for standard applications with international certification.
**Choose HP** for premium beam quality and higher reliability requirements.
**Choose ABP** when you process multiple material types/thicknesses and need beam versatility.

### QCW — For Precision Welding & Drilling
Use when you need high peak power in controlled pulses:
- Spot welding of electronics
- Precision drilling (0.1–1mm holes)
- Welding dissimilar metals
- Seam welding of thin materials

Peak power is 10x the average power — gives CW-like penetration with lower heat input.

### MOPA — For Marking & Micro-Processing
Use when you need tunable pulse characteristics:
- Color marking on stainless steel
- Black marking on anodized aluminum
- Fine engraving with depth control
- Selective surface texturing

The adjustable pulse width (2–500ns) is the key differentiator vs Q-switched.

### Q-Switched — For Standard Marking
Use for cost-effective marking and engraving:
- Serial numbers, barcodes, logos
- Date codes on production lines
- Part identification

Simpler and more affordable than MOPA when color marking isn't needed.

### High-Power Pulsed — For Cleaning
Use for surface preparation and cleaning:
- Rust and oxide removal
- Paint and coating stripping
- Weld preparation and post-weld cleaning
- Mold cleaning

## Power Selection Guide

| Cutting Application | Thickness | Recommended Power |
|---|---|---|
| Mild steel | 1–6mm | 1000–2000W |
| Mild steel | 6–12mm | 3000–6000W |
| Mild steel | 12–20mm | 6000–12000W |
| Mild steel | 20–40mm | 12000–30000W |
| Stainless steel | 1–4mm | 1000–2000W |
| Stainless steel | 4–10mm | 3000–6000W |
| Aluminum | 1–6mm | 2000–4000W |
| Aluminum | 6–16mm | 6000–12000W |
